name: Conjunctivitis (Pink Eye)

key_questions: Unilateral or bilateral? Note: bacterial conjunctivitis is also commonly bilateral — laterality alone is not a reliable discriminator. Better discriminators: mucopurulent discharge + morning lid matting → bacterial; watery + itch + bilateral + seasonal → allergic; watery + recent viral illness + pre-auricular lymphadenopathy → viral.
Contact lens wearer? — higher risk of Pseudomonas/serious infection — refer to physician/NP or ophthalmologist
Sexual history: gonorrhea risk (neonates, sexually active adults)?
red_flags: Significant eye pain or photophobia → keratitis, uveitis — urgent ophthalmology
Vision changes (blurring not cleared by blinking) → keratitis — urgent
Corneal opacity or infiltrate (white spot visible on cornea) → urgent
Contact lens wearer with purulent discharge → Pseudomonas risk — refer immediately
HSV keratitis: unilateral, dendritic ulcer pattern, history of cold sores or periocular vesicles — emergent referral to ophthalmology
ddx: Viral (adenovirus — most common): watery discharge, follicular, URI, pre-auricular node
Allergic: bilateral, intense itch, stringy discharge, seasonal
Bacterial: mucopurulent, morning lid matting, unilateral or bilateral
Subconjunctival hemorrhage (painless, bright red patch — benign)
Episcleritis (sectoral redness, mild ache, no discharge)

first_line.name: Bacterial Conjunctivitis — Topical Antibiotic
first_line.drugs: Tobramycin 0.3% drops (Tobrex) 1–2 drops q4h × 7 days | Erythromycin 0.5% ointment QID × 7 days | Fusidic acid 1% gel (Fucithalmic) BID × 7 days | Polymyxin B/gramicidin (Polysporin Eye & Ear Drops) 1–2 drops q3–4h × 7 days | Polymyxin B/trimethoprim (Polytrim) 1 drop q3h × 7–10 days
first_line.notes: Mild-moderate bacterial conjunctivitis is usually self-limiting (resolves within 7–10 days without treatment). Antibiotics shorten duration by 2–3 days. Fusidic acid gel: once or twice daily — most convenient. Tobramycin: broad coverage. Polymyxin B/gramicidin and polymyxin B/trimethoprim (Polytrim) are both on Ontario Schedule 4 and are appropriate first-line options. SCOPE NOTE: Ophthalmic fluoroquinolones (ofloxacin, ciprofloxacin, moxifloxacin) are OUTSIDE Ontario pharmacist MA prescribing scope. Contact lens wearers with purulent discharge MUST be referred to a physician/NP or ophthalmologist — do not prescribe under MA. Counsel patients to discontinue contact lens use during infection and replace lens case after recovery.
first_line.preferred_when: Moderate mucopurulent discharge, significant morning lid matting, immunocompromised

first_line.name: Allergic Conjunctivitis — Antihistamine Eye Drops
first_line.drugs: Olopatadine (Pataday) 1 drop OD | Ketotifen (Zaditor, Alaway) 1 drop BID
first_line.notes: Combination antihistamine/mast cell stabilizer drops: most effective for allergic conjunctivitis. SCOPE NOTE: Olopatadine (Pataday) and ketotifen (Zaditor, Alaway) are OTC products in Canada — pharmacist role is OTC recommendation and counselling, not MA prescribing. Olopatadine: once-daily (convenient). Ketotifen: OTC twice-daily. Significantly reduces itch and redness within minutes.
first_line.preferred_when: Allergic conjunctivitis (itch, bilateral, seasonal, stringy discharge)

second_line.name: Artificial Tears
second_line.notes: For viral conjunctivitis (self-limiting, no antibiotic benefit) and as adjunct for allergic. Cool compresses also helpful. Viral conjunctivitis is the most common type — supportive care and hand hygiene are the mainstay; antibiotics provide no benefit.

non_pharm: Warm compresses (bacterial) or cool compresses (allergic/viral) QID
Clean eyelids gently with warm water and clean cloth
Highly contagious (viral/bacterial): frequent handwashing, avoid sharing towels, pillow cases

School/daycare exclusion: current Ontario and CDC guidance does not routinely require exclusion if the child is receiving treatment and good hand hygiene is practiced — discuss with school/daycare on a case-by-case basis
Discard mascara/eye makeup used during infection
Contact lens wearers: discontinue lens use until infection resolved; replace lens case after recovery
refer_when: Contact lens wearer with conjunctivitis — refer to physician/NP or ophthalmologist (Pseudomonas risk; FQ required)
Vision changes or significant eye pain or photophobia — urgent ophthalmology
Suspected HSV keratitis (unilateral, dendritic pattern, periocular vesicles, cold sore history) — emergent
No improvement in 48–72 hours after starting topical antibiotic treatment for bacterial conjunctivitis

references: Ontario College of Pharmacists. Minor Ailments: Conjunctivitis. OCP Prescribing Standards, O. Reg. 256/24 (2025).
Canadian Ophthalmological Society (COS). Evidence-based Clinical Practice Guidelines for Conjunctivitis (2021).
AAO (American Academy of Ophthalmology). Preferred Practice Pattern: Conjunctivitis (2018, reviewed 2023).
NICE. Conjunctivitis — infective: Clinical Knowledge Summary (CKS). National Institute for Health and Care Excellence (2023).
